# Vendored font sources

## `font8x8_*.h` — 8x8 monochrome bitmap fonts

Author: Daniel Hepper `<daniel@hepper.net>`
Upstream: https://github.com/dhepper/font8x8
License: **Public Domain**

Quote from the header of each file:

> 8x8 monochrome bitmap fonts for rendering
> Author: Daniel Hepper <daniel@hepper.net>
> License: Public Domain
>
> Based on:
>     Marcel Sondaar
>     International Business Machines (public domain VGA fonts)
> License:
>     Public Domain

These files are vendored verbatim from
`https://github.com/dhepper/font8x8` (commit HEAD as of the import
date) so a source diff stays tractable. The conversion to Rust const
data lives under `src/fonts/*.rs` and is generated by
`tools/gen_fonts.py` (a plain text parser — one-shot, output
committed).

### Bit layout (font8x8)

Each glyph is 8 bytes, one byte per row. Within a byte, **bit 0 is
the leftmost pixel** (LSB-first). The `BitmapFont::bit_order` field
carries `BitOrder::Lsb` for these and preserves the convention
downstream into the upload + draw paths.

## `IBM_VGA_8x16.bin` — 8×16 IBM VGA BIOS font

Upstream: https://github.com/spacerace/romfont (path
`font-bin/IBM_VGA_8x16.bin`, extracted from an IBM VGA BIOS
ROM dump).
License of bitmap data: **Public Domain**.

This is the canonical 1981 IBM VGA character-generator ROM content
— the same data that ships in every VGA-era PC BIOS and can be
found in hundreds of projects (Linux `font_8x16.c`, FreeDOS
kernel, dhepper's font8x8 series, int10h.org's "oldschool PC
fonts," etc.). The bitmap bytes themselves have no enforceable
copyright claim (IBM published the exact glyph bitmaps in the
PC/AT technical reference manuals and made no proprietary claim at
the time of VGA's 1987 release).

Format: 256 glyphs × 16 bytes each, one byte per row, **MSB-first**
within each byte (bit 7 = leftmost pixel, matching IBM convention).
Total 4096 bytes.

### Bit layout (IBM VGA 8×16)

8-pixel-wide glyphs → one byte per row. `BitmapFont::bit_order` is
set to `BitOrder::Msb` for this font; the uploader's row-unpacker
(see [`BitmapFont::glyph_row_packed`]) normalizes both LSB and MSB
sources to the same internal representation.

## Why these instead of e.g. Linux `font_*.c`

Linux's kernel fbcon fonts are GPL-2.0 licensed even though the
bitmap data itself is universally considered PD. Shipping under a
plain PD grant via projects dedicated to PD redistribution is
simpler to reason about for this GPL-licensed repo. The visual output
is the same IBM-VGA look — these are all derivatives of the same
1981 IBM character-generator ROM.
